items.reality: Conflicts mean Git is being responsible. The three-way merge can't read your mind — when both sides changed the same lines, it asks. A conflict is a safety feature.
items.example:
```
# Both branches set timeout = something_different.
# Git refuses to guess — that's the correct behaviour.
```

items.myth: You should always pick HEAD (your side).
items.reality: HEAD is *not* always 'yours' — during a rebase the meaning flips. Read the markers carefully. Better: resolve by *intent*, not by side.
items.example:
```
# During merge: HEAD = the branch you're merging INTO (usually 'yours').
# During rebase: HEAD = the branch being replayed onto, so the meaning of 'ours' / 'theirs' swaps.
```

items.myth: Auto-merge means there are no conflicts to worry about.
items.reality: Auto-merge only handles non-overlapping line-level changes. Two refactors can auto-merge to code that doesn't even compile — a 'semantic conflict' Git can't see.
items.example:
```python
# Branch A renames the function `decode` to `decode_v2`.
# Branch B adds a new caller of `decode(x)`.
# Both auto-merge; the result fails at runtime.
```

items.myth: Aborting a merge or rebase loses your changes.
items.reality: `--abort` returns to the *pre-merge* / *pre-rebase* state — your working tree's committed work is intact. Only the in-progress operation is undone.
items.example:
```bash
git merge feature/auth
# ... overwhelmed by conflicts ...
git merge --abort   # back to a clean state, can try again later
```
